learning_outcomes.description: Understand the nature and process of research and familiarise yourself with different research areas in language and culture studies
Comprehend the basic aspects and components of research methodology, from the inception of a research project to data collection, analysis, and presentation
Critically review, analyse, and synthesise findings from a range of secondary sources
Design and present your research proposal in a manner conforming to accepted academic standards in both written and spoken form
Identify, locate, and effectively use research materials relevant to your field of study

assessments.task_description:
The Research Methods Learning Journal is a reflective assessment designed to support your ongoing engagement with course content and encourage the gradual development of your understanding of research design, methodology, and ethical practice in Applied Linguistics.

Across the semester, you are required to submit five short reflective journal entries, each based on a selected session of the course. Each entry should be between 100 and 200 words in length and is worth 4% of your final grade, contributing a total of 20%.

Important: Students may submit more than five entries; however, only the first five entries submitted (based on submission date and time) will be marked.

Purpose

The purpose of this assessment is to promote regular reflection on your learning and to support you in connecting theoretical discussions of research methods to your own developing research interests. Through these reflections, you will consolidate your understanding of research design, identify areas of uncertainty, and consider how different methodological approaches may be applied to empirical research in Applied Linguistics.

What Sessions Can I Write About?

You may write a learning journal entries based on the discussions held in any tutorial session between WEEK 3 and WEEK 12. You choose which five sessions to reflect on—they do not need to be consecutive or cover all topics.

Key point: If you were unable to attend a particular tutorial due to illness or other circumstances, you can write about a different session instead. However, if you do submit an entry about a session you did not attend, you will receive an automatic deduction of 15% on that entry's grade (0.6% off the final grade). This is to encourage in-person attendance and reward genuine tutorial engagement.

Tutors take note of attendance each week and will verify whether you were present when marking your entries.

Deadlines and Submission

Each journal entry must be submitted by Monday 1:00 PM of the week following the session it reflects on.

NOTE: The Week 10 journal is due on Tuesday 1:00 PM due to the King's Birthday public holiday on Monday.

Deadline Examples

  • Week 3 tutorial → Entry due Monday of Week 4 at 1:00 PM
  • Week 6 tutorial → Entry due Monday of Week 7 at 1:00 PM
  • Week 12 tutorial → Entry due Monday of Week 13 at 1:00 PM

How to Submit

  • Submit via Blackboard in the designated Learning Journal submission box
  • Entries must be submitted as a Word document or PDF
  • Include your name and student ID at the top of your document

What Should My Entry Contain?

Each journal entry should reflect on your learning from a particular session and engage meaningfully with the content covered. Entries should be specific, detailed, and substantive—not superficial or overly general.

Consider:

  • What did you learn about research methods or research design in this session?
  • Which methodological approaches or analytical techniques were introduced?
  • What aspects did you find challenging, unclear, or particularly interesting?
  • How could the concepts discussed inform the design of a research project in Applied Linguistics?
  • Did you make connections to your own research interests or previous learning?

Not all aspects need to be addressed in every entry. However, entries should demonstrate thoughtful, reflective, and substantive engagement with the session content.

Avoid generic, superficial entries that lack specific references to the session or fail to go beyond description. Good entries make explicit connections and show genuine reflection.

Word Count Requirement

Your entry must be between 100 and 200 words. This is a strict requirement:

  • Under 100 words: Downgraded for insufficient development
  • Over 200 words: Downgraded for lack of conciseness

Use a word counter to check your entry before submitting. Every word counts!

Generative AI and Machine Translation

These journal entries test YOUR genuine reflection on YOUR learning. Artificial intelligence cannot reflect for you. Use of AI to generate the entry itself (rather than just to clarify concepts during preparation) is not appropriate for this task.

If you use AI during your preparation to understand concepts, this must be clearly acknowledged. However, remember that any acknowledgement of AI use eats into your 100-200 word limit. So the more AI you use, the fewer words you have for your actual reflection.

A failure to acknowledge AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. If you pass this assessment, you demonstrate that you have genuinely engaged with course content and reflected on your own learning.

assessments.task_description:
In this assessment, you will work in groups of 2 to 4 students to deliver a 30-minute in-class presentation based on an assigned chapter from the textbook Research Methods in Applied Linguistics (Phakiti & Paltridge, Eds.).

Unlike the core tutorial reading for the week, your presentation chapter will be a different one—chosen to broaden the focus of the course and expose students to a wider range of methods and issues in applied linguistics research. This task is designed to deepen your engagement with course content, enhance your academic communication skills, and foster collaborative learning.

Topic and Scheduling

  • Each group will present on a specific chapter that complements the weekly tutorial topic but is not the same as the chapter used in the tutorial.
  • A sign-up Padlet with available presentation chapters will be shared with students in WEEK 2 and 3.
  • Groups will sign up to present in one tutorial session between WEEK 4 and WEEK 12.
  • Presentations begin in WEEK 4 and continue through to Friday, WEEK 12.

Presentation Format

  • Each presentation must run for 30 minutes and should include:
  • ~20–25 minutes summarising the key ideas, methods, and insights from the assigned chapter.
  • One in-class task or activity (~5–10 minutes) designed to actively engage your peers with the chapter’s content.
  • Presentations will be delivered in class and recorded for moderation.
  • You may choose any appropriate presentation format (e.g., lecture-style, interactive, multimedia), but visual aids (slides, handouts, etc.) are strongly encouraged.

Submission Requirements

  • All groups must submit a single PDF via Turnitin at least 2 hours before the scheduled presentation.
  • The PDF must include
  • all presentation materials (slides, handouts, supporting docs).
  • A self-assessment outlining each member’s contributions
  • A brief peer assessment reflecting on group collaboration

Note: All group members receive the same grade unless exceptional circumstances apply. Self- and peer-assessments are required for transparency and accountability.

Alternative Assessment

Students unable to participate in the in-class group presentation due to illness or approved special circumstances must still submit the required documents. An alternative individual assessment will be arranged.

Assessment Criteria

Your presentation will be assessed based on:

  • Accuracy and depth of coverage of the assigned chapter
  • Clarity, structure, and organisation of the presentation
  • Communication and delivery quality
  • Effectiveness of engagement during the in-class task

A detailed marking rubric will be provided on Blackboard (Ultra).

Weighting

This task is worth 30% of your final course grade.

Feedback

  • Written feedback about the presentation will be provided on the materials submitted via Turnitin.

Statement on Generative AI & Machine Translation (MT)

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

assessments.assessment_title: Research Ethics and Academic Integrity Interview
assessments.due_date:
21/09/2026 - 9/10/2026

Interviews will be held between WEEK 9 and WEEK 10, and scheduled outside of class time.
assessments.task_description:
In this assessment, you will participate in a 10-minute individual interview designed to evaluate your understanding of research ethics, integrity, and best practices in applied linguistics research. The interview takes the form of a dialogue in which you will be presented with a realistic research scenario containing ethical, methodological, and/or integrity issues. Your task is to identify these issues and propose practical solutions.

This is a hurdle assessment: you must achieve a pass to pass the course. However, this assessment does not contribute to your final grade—it is designed to ensure that all students in the course have engaged meaningfully with research ethics and academic integrity content.

Rationale for this assessment

  • Develops critical thinking about real-world research dilemmas
  • Tests genuine understanding of course content (not memorisation)
  • Ensures all graduates understand their ethical responsibilities as researchers
  • Prepares you for ethical decision-making in future research roles

Scenario and Scheduling

Five realistic research scenarios will be made available on Blackboard in WEEK 8. You should study and familiarize yourself with all five scenarios before your interview—you will not know which scenario you will be asked about.

Scenario characteristics

  • Each scenario is specific to applied linguistics research and presents realistic dilemmas
  • Each scenario contains at least one clear/glaring issue plus more subtle issues to identify
  • Scenarios cover mixed research designs (qualitative, quantitative, mixed methods)
  • Two scenarios specifically address indigenous language and culture contexts to reflect the diversity of applied linguistics work globally (e.g., teaching English to Aboriginal students in remote Australia; recruiting indigenous participants for language research)
  • All scenarios are similar in complexity and difficulty

You may

  • Read the five scenarios during WEEK 8–WEEK 9
  • Discuss scenarios with peers informally
  • Review them on your own terms

You may not

  • Bring any materials, notes, or references into the interview
  • Use any electronic devices during the interview

Interview Timing

  • Ethics content delivered: WEEK 8 (Research Ethics and Academic Integrity session)
  • Scenario availability: WEEK 8 onward on Blackboard
  • Interview scheduling: WEEK 8 and WEEK9
  • Sign-up: A Blackboard sign-up link will be announced in tutorials in WEEKS 8 and 9

Select a time slot that suits your schedule. Interviews are typically 10–15 minutes and will be scheduled across multiple days in WEEK 9 and WEEK 10 to accommodate all students.

Interview Format

Structure

Your 10-minute interview will consist of:

  • ~1–2 minutes: Examiner reads/summarises a scenario
  • ~6–8 minutes: Dialogue in which you identify issues and propose solutions
  • ~1 minute: Examiner provides verbal feedback

The scenario will be randomly selected from the five available scenarios—you will not know in advance which one you will be asked about.

Format: Dialogue-Based Discussion

The interview is not a formal presentation. Instead, it is a conversation:

  • The examiner will present the scenario
  • You will be asked to identify issues (ethical, methodological, or integrity-related)
  • The examiner may ask clarifying questions or prompt you
  • You should explain your reasoning and propose practical solutions
  • The examiner will summarise your engagement and provide verbal feedback

This is a supportive, low-stakes environment. The goal is to assess whether you have engaged meaningfully with course content—not to trick you or require perfect answers.

What You MUST Bring

  • Valid student ID (required for identity verification)
  • Nothing else

You may NOT bring

  • Notes, study guides, or reference materials
  • Electronic devices (laptop, phone, tablet)
  • Handwritten notes
  • Any other aids

Recording and Identity Verification

  • Your interview will be recorded for moderation and quality assurance purposes
  • The recording will be stored securely and used only for assessment verification
  • You must bring a valid student ID (university card, passport, or other official ID) to verify your identity at the start of the interview
  • Recording begins after identity verification is complete

Assessment Criteria: What is "Competent Engagement"?

To achieve a pass, you must demonstrate competent engagement, which means:

1. Issue Identification (Core)

You should identify at least one clear issue and attempt to identify more subtle ones:

  • Ethical issues: Consent, confidentiality, conflict of interest, cultural sensitivity, researcher responsibility
  • Methodological flaws: Validity threats, sampling bias, reliability concerns, design limitations
  • Integrity concerns: Fabrication, falsification, misrepresentation, selective reporting, plagiarism

You do not need to identify every issue, but you should demonstrate that you understand the types of issues that exist.

2. Understanding of Issues (Core)

You should explain why each identified issue is problematic:

  • "This is an ethical concern because..."
  • "This could compromise the validity of findings because..."
  • "This violates academic integrity because..."

Vague or incorrect reasoning = may not constitute competent engagement.

3. Proposed Solutions (Core)

You should propose practical, realistic solutions for at least one identified issue:

  • "To address this, the researcher could..."
  • "A better approach would be..."
  • "The researcher should have..."

Solutions do not need to be perfect, but they should show you understand how to mitigate issues.

4. Engagement with Course Content (Supporting)

Your discussion should reflect concepts and principles covered in the course:

  • Reference to informed consent, confidentiality, cultural sensitivity
  • Awareness of research design principles
  • Understanding of researcher responsibility and integrity
  • Sensitivity to vulnerable populations or power dynamics

Pass/Fail Criteria

PASS

You will achieve a pass if you:

  • Identify at least one clear issue (usually obvious from the scenario)
  • Demonstrate understanding of why it's problematic
  • Propose at least one practical solution
  • Show that you have engaged meaningfully with the research ethics and academic integrity content from WEEK 8
  • Communicate clearly enough for examiners to understand your reasoning

FAIL

You will achieve a fail only if:

  • You cannot identify any issues in the scenario, OR
  • Your reasoning demonstrates fundamental misunderstanding of research ethics/integrity principles, OR
  • You show no evidence of having engaged with the course content on research ethics and academic integrity

Failure indicates you have not done the preparatory work (reading scenarios, attending WEEK 8 ethics session, thinking about ethical issues).

Feedback

Verbal feedback will be provided immediately after the interview:

  • You will receive an overall assessment: PASS or FAIL
  • Examiners will summarise your strengths (e.g., "You identified the consent issue quickly and proposed a good solution")
  • Examiners will note areas for future consideration (e.g., "Next time, think about cultural considerations with vulnerable populations")
  • This feedback is conversational and supportive, not evaluative

You will not receive a numerical grade, as this is a hurdle, not a graded assessment

Generative AI and Machine Translation

This assessment has been designed to test genuine understanding of course content that generative AI cannot prepare you for.

While you may use AI and other tools to study the scenarios beforehand (e.g., to understand concepts, explore definitions), the interview itself tests your independent comprehension and reasoning.

Important

  • You cannot bring any notes or other materials (e.g., AI-generated notes) into the interview.
  • The interview is a dialogue in which examiners assess your thinking in real time.
  • Memorising AI-generated summaries is unlikely to help—you must understand the underlying principles.
  • If you pass this assessment, you demonstrate that you have genuinely engaged with research ethics content, independent of AI support.

The interview is designed to verify each student's individual understanding of the ethical principles and responsibilities required of researchers.

Students are expected to complete this assessment honestly and authentically, in accordance with the UQ Student Code of Conduct and Academic Integrity Policy. Failure to do so may result in the matter being considered under the University's academic integrity or student conduct procedures.

assessments.task_description:
For this assessment, you will write a research proposal of no more than 2000 words (excluding references) that outlines the design and significance of an original research project in Applied Linguistics. The proposal must be submitted as a single Word or PDF document via Turnitin on Blackboard.

The proposal should clearly demonstrate your ability to conceptualise, design, and reflect on a feasible research project that contributes meaningfully to a topic in Applied Linguistics.

Purpose

This task enables you to:

  • Investigate a specific, researchable topic in Applied Linguistics in depth
  • Demonstrate your understanding of relevant literature, theories, and research methods
  • Formulate a clear research question and design an appropriate methodology
  • Reflect critically on the feasibility, significance, and limitations of your project

Your research proposal should present a project that is original and well-justified, with a clear focus and scholarly grounding.

Structure and Content

Your proposal should include the following sections:

1. Introduction

  • Introduce your research topic and area of investigation
  • Establish the significance and relevance of the study
  • Outline the structure of the proposal

2. Literature Review

  • Provide a clear and well-organised synthesis of key literature related to your topic
  • Identify theoretical perspectives, major findings, and gaps in existing research
  • Demonstrate your understanding of key concepts and scholarly debates

3. Research Question

  • Clearly state your research question(s) or hypothesis
  • Justify your research focus in relation to the literature review

4. Methodology

  • Provide a detailed description of your proposed research design
  • Include information on:
  • Research context and participants (if applicable)
  • Data collection methods and instruments
  • Data analysis procedures
  • Ensure the approach is ethical, appropriate, and feasible

5. Conclusion

  • Critically reflect on the contribution and significance of your proposed study
  • Identify any limitations or anticipated challenges

6. References

  • Include a complete and accurate reference list in APA 7th edition format

Word Limit

  • Maximum: 2000 words (excluding references)
  • A 10% leeway (1800–2200 words) is acceptable

Submission

  • Submit a single Word or PDF file via Turnitin on Blackboard
  • Due date: Tuesday, Exam WEEK 1 by 4pm (AEST)

Assessment Criteria

Your proposal will be assessed based on the following:

  • Relevance and clarity of the research topic and question
  • Depth, coherence, and critical engagement in the literature review
  • Appropriateness and feasibility of the research design
  • Critical reflection on the study’s contribution and limitations
  • Clarity, organisation, and academic writing quality
  • Accurate referencing using APA 7 style

A detailed rubric will be available on Blackboard.

Generative AI and Machine Translation Statement

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
